title: Who’s Afraid of Chinese Models?
excerpt: Everyone is worried about Chinese models, but the frontier labs will be fine; we need to enable open U.S. alternatives.
content_html:
Listen to this post:

There’s a story I tell about my first day in STRT-431 at Kellogg School of Management, the introductory class that every first-year MBA was required to take; I leafed through the readings and case studies and was dismayed that there weren’t any tech companies on the docket. Me being me, I spoke to the professor after class wondering why, and was told that the goal of the course was not to necessarily learn about specific industries, but rather to uncover broadly applicable universal principles that could be applied to any company in any industry.

I did not, as I usually tell the story, find this very satisfactory: to me the nature of tech, particularly the fact that software and distribution had zero marginal costs (and zero transaction costs), was something fundamentally different; putting in zeroes in formulas tends to wreak havoc! I soon realized, however, that that was my opportunity. The fundamental insight undergirding Aggregation Theory is that zero marginal costs leads to fundamentally different value chains than people once expected from the Internet: centralization and scale in a world where controlling demand mattered more than distributing supply.

What is fascinating about AI, however, is the extent to which those old universal principles are coming back to the forefront. That was never more apparent than this past weekend, when arguments raged on X about the implications of Kimi K3, another open weights model out of China, approaching the state-of-the-art in terms of capabilities. The long and short of it is this: marginal costs are back in a big way, both in terms of short-term implications of state-of-the-art free models, and in terms of the long-term structure of the industry.

COGS Versus R&D

One of the most common misconceptions undergirding discussion of open weights models is that they are cheaper — free, even. After all, you can just download the weights, and skip the time and expense and capabilities necessary to create your own model. That is, of course, true, but the “free” in this case is a reference to the amount you need to spend on research and development; R&D is a fixed expense that is independent of the revenue you generate. If you spend $1 million in R&D, it doesn’t matter if you do $100 thousand in revenue or $100 million; you still spent $1 million on R&D (it does, of course, impact your profitability).

What is related to revenue is COGS — cost of goods sold — and COGS is real for AI in a way it hasn’t been for software for a very long time. Specifically, running inference on a model — whether that model be Kimi or Fable — costs money, and the amount of money an AI provider spends on inference is, at least in most business models, directly correlated to revenue. To reuse the above example, generating $100 million versus $100 thousand in revenue will likely require 1,000x COGS. In concrete terms, if it costs 50 cents to generate the tokens that drive $1 in revenue, then $100 million in revenue will have $50 million in COGS; $100 thousand in revenue will only have $50 thousand in COGS.

The point in terms of open weight models is that they are not free to serve. Kimi K3 costs $3 per million input tokens, and $15 per million output tokens; that is cheaper than Sol’s $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, but that might not even be the right measurement.

Tokens Versus Intelligence

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has described what Nvidia is building as “token factories”, and from Nvidia’s perspective that framing makes sense. Nvidia GPUs are model agnostic: they generate tokens, and do so in the fastest and most efficient way possible. That leads to measurements like tokens-per-second, time-to-first-token, tokens-per-watt, token cost, etc., and Huang argues that these metrics will be the basis for decision-making.

This is a framing that definitely made sense during the first paradigm of AI, the ChatGPT era, when tokens were delivered straight to the end user. The second paradigm of AI, however, the reasoning era, confounds this measurement. Reasoning entails an explosion in chain-of-thought tokens, and different models need different amounts of reasoning tokens to arrive at the right answer. Kimi, for example, reportedly uses significantly more tokens than Sol, rendering its price advantage moot. Agents introduce a similar dynamic: some models are more efficient than others in terms of the number of tokens they need to execute agentic workflows.

What this means is that tokens are not a commodity. The defining characteristic of a commodity is that it is fungible: a gallon of oil is a gallon of oil; a ton of copper is a ton of copper; a bushel of wheat is a bushel of wheat. A token from one model, however, is not the same as a token from another model. What is fungible is what is constructed from tokens, which is to say intelligence. In other words, if both Kimi and Sol generated the right answer, then that answer is fungible; the difference in tokens generated to get to that right answer is a contributor to a difference in COGS.

The COGS for intelligence is a function of a few different factors:

  • Model footprint: The weights and runtime state determine how much expensive memory and how many accelerators are required to host each serving replica.
  • Inference efficiency: Architectural choices (e.g. Mixture-of-Experts) reduce computation per generated token.
  • Memory efficiency: Architectural choices can reduce KV cache requirements, allowing more concurrent requests and better GPU utilization.
  • Serving efficiency: Batching, scheduling, prefix caching, and other inference optimizations maximize utilization and share work across requests.
  • Token efficiency: The fewer tokens required to reach a correct answer, the lower the inference cost.

The reason this matters is that we are rapidly approaching a state in which intelligence for many economically beneficial tasks is in fact a commodity. Anyone building a basic CRUD app, for example, can likely do so using models from multiple providers. And, in a commodity market, the route to profitability is not through charging higher prices — again, you can (or will soon be able to) make the exact same app using multiple models — but rather through having a superior cost structure.

Understanding Commodity Markets

It’s worth stepping through the mechanics here, because, as I noted a few months ago in Amazon’s Durability, the dynamics of commodity markets are not something people in tech are generally familiar with:

  • In commodity markets, everyone charges the same price, because everyone is selling the same thing; that price is determined by supply and demand.
  • The demand for a commodity is a function of price elasticity: the cheaper the commodity, the more demand there is for it, and vice-versa.
  • The supply for a commodity is a function of the marginal cost of producing the commodity.

The key thing to understand is that the marginal cost of producing the commodity differs by supplier. What this means in practice is that the supplier with the worst cost structure ends up selling the commodity at their marginal cost (if they can produce at all); the profits of everyone else depend on the extent to which their cost structure is better than the marginal supplier.

As an example:

  • Supplier A can produce 10 units of the commodity for $10 each
  • Supplier B can produce 10 units of the commodity for $15 each
  • Supplier C can produce 10 units of the commodity for $20 each

Let’s assume the price elasticity is such that there is demand for 25 units of the commodity at $20. That means:

  • Supplier A will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $10/unit
  • Supplier B will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $5/unit
  • Supplier C will sell 5 units of the commodity for $20, earning $0/unit

This isn’t precisely right: the reason why Supplier C will bear the shortfall is because Suppliers A and B will be able to slightly undercut them in price, which will of course affect demand (which is elastic), but it makes the point. Supplier A has a great business, Supplier B has a good business, and Supplier C is going to go bankrupt.

Bankruptcy risk is where fixed costs come back to the forefront: Supplier C has both fixed costs (like potentially R&D spend) and also may have taken on debt to finance the equipment necessary to produce the commodity. It can’t price its commodity with these costs in mind — remember, the market-clearing price approximates the marginal cost of the highest-cost unit needed to satisfy demand — but those costs can absolutely drive the supplier out of business. And, if that supplier goes out of business, then prices go up, until another supplier decides to enter (or the other suppliers expand).

The Intelligence Market

Let’s bring this back to models. Right now, none of the above analysis applies because demand exceeds supply for frontier models, and supply is limited by a lack of compute. This compute shortage doesn’t just mean that a compute supplier like Nvidia makes very large margins, but also that Nvidia’s customers, like SpaceXAI, can turn around and resell compute at high margins as well to a company like Anthropic. Anthropic, meanwhile, can pay the markup because they can sell tokens with a higher markup still.

It’s not just excess demand that gives Anthropic great margins, however: Anthropic and OpenAI likely have among the lowest costs per unit of frontier-quality intelligence, thanks to model capability, serving scale, and token efficiency. They are serving models at a particular capability level for months before their competitors, and are simultaneously applying the best models to optimizing those costs.

It’s also worth noting that the market is not yet treating intelligence like a commodity: demand is for Anthropic and OpenAI specifically, and much less for models that aren’t as good (thus SpaceXAI and Meta selling capacity to Anthropic); one way to think about the push for optimizing cost is that that is a function of defining jobs-to-be-done by intelligence level, such that intelligence buyers can create a market where intelligence is commoditized. In the long run, however, whoever is on the frontier is the best placed to dominate non-frontier markets as well, which are just the frontier minus n-months, i.e. months in which the frontier model makers have been optimizing their cost of serving.

All of this is to say that I think the reaction to Kimi and Chinese models generally is pretty over-blown, at least from an economic perspective. Right now there is a price umbrella that is downstream of the lack of compute; I highly doubt that Chinese models are cheaper to serve on a marginal cost basis, they just seem cheaper because Anthropic and OpenAI are so supply constrained that they are charging far more than they would if there were sufficient supply to meet the demand for intelligence.

Frontier Lab Paranoia

Why, then, do the model makers in particular seem so panicked about Chinese models?

First, I think the frontier labs are anchored in a world where training costs dominated their financial modeling. As long as training consumed more GPUs than inference, it was critical to maximize inference revenue to help fund the next training run, which meant charging very high prices for inference.

Going forward, however, I expect the inference market to grow much faster than training costs (and that includes the assumption that training costs will continue to skyrocket), which means they really can make it up in volume. It wasn’t clear this would be the case as recently as eight months ago, but the agent paradigm unlock is so massive that frontier labs should have more confidence that they can not just survive but thrive with lower prices (once they have sufficient compute).

Second, intelligence isn’t in fact a perfect commodity, in part because applied intelligence makes itself smarter. Specifically, whoever is running inference is also collecting data, and that data goes into making the next iteration of the model better. This is, on one hand, all the more reason for the frontier labs to lower prices and increase usage as more compute comes online; on the other hand, this is why companies like Microsoft are increasingly obsessed with helping companies run their own models. That is much more viable if Chinese models are a viable alternative.

Third, the other way that frontier labs can not only differentiate from Chinese models but also from each other is by continuing to integrate up into the customer experience. It’s striking the extent to which Claude Code and Codex are proving to be quite sticky; whichever harness you start working with is likely to be the one you stick with, and that figures to be even more the case with non-technical users. And, in the long run, this imperative to move up the stack does mean that frontier models are absolutely a threat to software providers, including Microsoft. On the flipside, the extent to which software companies who currently own the customer experience have access to competitive models is the extent to which they may be able to resist the encroachment of the frontier labs.

Finally, the ideological angle of Anthropic in particular is impossible to ignore. This is a company that believes only it can be entrusted with AI, and the existence of open weights alternatives strikes a fatal blow to that presumption.

China’s Motivation

Kimi isn’t the only new Chinese model; from Bloomberg:

Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. shares rose as much as 5.4% on Monday after the company launched a preview version of its flagship Qwen3.8 Max model, describing it as second only to Anthropic PBC’s Fable 5. The Sunday release came only days after startup Moonshot AI unveiled a powerful new offering that’s roiled markets and triggered concern in the US about China closing the gap on global leaders like Anthropic and OpenAI. Qwen3.8 Max has 2.4 trillion parameters, joining Moonshot’s Kimi K3 in the heavyweight class. With 2.8 trillion parameters, K3 rivals top offerings and Alibaba is setting similarly high expectations.

Developers can now access Qwen3.8 Max through Alibaba’s coding platforms, including Qoder. Alibaba plans to make the model open-weight soon, expanding access beyond the preview release. Interest in these made-in-China artificial intelligence systems and models is so high that Moonshot was forced to pause taking on new subscriptions late on Sunday to manage overwhelming demand.

The fact that Qwen3.8 Max will also have open weights is notable. Alibaba stopped releasing weights for its leading edge models earlier this year, but appears to have reverted that change; I suspect that shift was related to last week’s Xi Jinping speech about AI that doubled down on the open weights approach:

We should adhere to the principle of openness and win-win and boost innovation-driven development. As a new engine of world economic growth and an accelerator for the shift of growth drivers, AI is moving from the digital world into the physical world. We should seize this rare, historic opportunity to encourage open source, openness, collaboration and sharing. We should facilitate technological innovation, industrial development and scenario-based application of AI. We should make coordinated advances in the transformation and upgrade of traditional industries, the cultivation and growth of emerging industries and forward-looking planning for future industries, so that all sectors and businesses can benefit from AI.

The strategy for China is obvious: commoditize your complements. Note that Xi explicitly ties openness to AI “moving from the digital world into the physical world”; the physical world is the world dominated by China, and the country’s lead in areas like robotics is going to massively benefit from widely available AI models.

Along the same lines, China does not want the U.S. to gain an asymmetric advantage in AI; to the extent that China can weaken the U.S. frontier labs while strengthening any and all potential U.S. adversaries so much the better, and it can benefit from the innovation that will attach itself to an open ecosystem.

The Distillation Question

By the same token, don’t expect China to do anything about distillation attacks on the frontier labs. I think it is mistaken to attribute all of the success of Chinese labs to distillation, but it’s just as much of a mistake to pretend like distillation doesn’t give Chinese labs a big advantage. That advantage has really come to bear in the last year as post-training reinforcement learning has become increasingly crucial to model performance. Instead of having to fashion reinforcement learning environments from scratch, Chinese labs can simply use frontier labs models as teachers, allowing for rapid improvement at much lower costs (this is not the only reason why Chinese models are cheaper to develop, but it’s a big one).

What is interesting is that one of the most important use cases for Chinese models in the West is itself distillation. Thinking Machines, for example, which just released an open-weight model, relies on Chinese models to solve the cold start problem for reinforcement learning. Dean Meyer and Konstantine Buhler wrote an excellent article on X explaining that distillation means that Western open weight models are fundamentally disadvantaged relative to China:

Distillation does not explain China’s entire open-model lead. Chinese labs have world-class researchers, substantial compute, strong pre-trained models, software-hardware codesign, and rapidly improving post-training capabilities. But distillation compresses the costly final gap between a strong base and a near-frontier system. Even if distillation represents a smaller share of a Chinese model’s total capability, it represents a meaningful share of its advantage over American open models.

New enforcement mechanisms will make large-scale distillation harder, slower, and more expensive for Chinese companies. However, enforcement will not eliminate distillation backed by state actors. Every Western frontier advance therefore creates another teacher for Chinese labs. Western builders must either reproduce those capabilities independently or wait to learn from Chinese models. This gap gives Chinese labs a recurring structural advantage over Western companies.

This is a point that bears repeating: because U.S. open weight model makers must follow the frontier labs’ terms of service, they (1) are worse than Chinese alternatives and (2) end up distilling the distillation, just with a detour through Chinese labs. Wouldn’t it be better if western open weight model makers could go to the source?

To that end, here’s an even more interesting question around distillation: why exactly is it bad? After all, what are large language models but the distillation of all of the knowledge on the open Internet, scraped by the frontier labs and distilled into the models that are themselves being distilled? Who is exactly being wronged here?

In fact, this paradox is the solution. I believe that open weight models are good for innovation (and, per the above, I think that labs on the frontier will be fine), but it’s a problem to be dependent on China. The U.S. should pass a law that (1) makes explicit that collecting data for training models is fair use, and (2) bars terms of service that forbid distillation, for U.S. companies at a minimum. Stopping distillation — which is literally just querying the API — is nearly impossible; the U.S. should go the other way and lean into a new copyright policy that both indemnifies the labs and also guarantees that what they learned fuels further innovation for everyone else.

The Reason to Be Afraid

This entire Article has been an exercise in defusing overreaction to Kimi K3 specifically and Chinese open weight models generally; however, there is one reason to be concerned, and that is cybersecurity. Consider this story from The Stack:

Hugging Face said its production infrastructure was breached by an “autonomous” AI agent system early last week. The platform’s security team were initially stymied in their incident response (IR) by unnamed US LLM frontier model guardrails “which cannot distinguish an incident responder from an attacker,” they said. So Hugging Face’s defenders turned instead to the open-source GLM 5.2 model from China’s Z.ai lab – running it on their own infrastructure to analyse the 17,000+ logs, or footprints, that the attackers left behind.

That’s a striking public admission for the New York-headquartered Hugging Face, which lets users collaborate on models, datasets and applications, and which this summer hit the $100 million ARR mark. In an incident report, the company recommended that defenders “have a capable model you can run on your own infrastructure [our italics] vetted and ready before an incident, both to avoid guardrail lockout and to keep attacker data and credentials from leaving your environment.”

It’s difficult to overstate how wrong-headed the Trump administration’s panicked response to Anthropic’s release of Fable was, particularly since it exacerbated Anthropic’s worst tendencies in terms of assuming only they can be trusted with powerful AI. In a world with only one AI, it might make sense to reserve the most powerful cybersecurity capabilities for the U.S. government and trusted allies; however, that’s not the world we live in.

There are and will be models eminently capable of mounting cybersecurity attacks on existing infrastructure, and those models will be — already are — widely available. The best defense — the only viable defense, in fact — will be to make sure defenders have access to the best models as well. Right now defenders are effectively banned from using Fable or Sol for cybersecurity because of Trump administration directives; that means the best alternative is using models from a country which has been trying to weaken our cyber defenses for years. This is insane!

The better course is clear: first, loosen Fable and Sol restrictions on cybersecurity, and second, ensure that U.S. open weight model makers are on an equal playing field with China. Yes, the frontier labs will kick and scream about this, but the Administration should realize that listening to their histrionics has led the U.S. to a position where U.S. companies are dependent on China for their defenses. Let the frontier labs win by being better; don’t let them define safety or security, or pull up the ladder of humanity’s collective knowledge. China is already hard enough to compete with; letting them carry the standard for openness and innovation is simply giving away our biggest advantage.

Qwen3.8 Max has 2.4 trillion parameters, joining Moonshot’s Kimi K3 in the heavyweight class. With 2.8 trillion parameters, K3 rivals top offerings and Alibaba is setting similarly high expectations. Developers can now access Qwen3.8 Max through Alibaba’s coding platforms, including Qoder. Alibaba plans to make the model open-weight soon, expanding access beyond the preview release. Interest in these made-in-China artificial intelligence systems and models is so high that Moonshot was forced to pause taking on new subscriptions late on Sunday to manage overwhelming demand. The fact that Qwen3.8 Max will also have open weights is notable. Alibaba stopped releasing weights for its leading edge models earlier this year, but appears to have reverted that change; I suspect that shift was related to last week’s Xi Jinping speech about AI that doubled down on the open weights approach: We should adhere to the principle of openness and win-win and boost innovation-driven development. As a new engine of world economic growth and an accelerator for the shift of growth drivers, AI is moving from the digital world into the physical world. We should seize this rare, historic opportunity to encourage open source, openness, collaboration and sharing. We should facilitate technological innovation, industrial development and scenario-based application of AI. We should make coordinated advances in the transformation and upgrade of traditional industries, the cultivation and growth of emerging industries and forward-looking planning for future industries, so that all sectors and businesses can benefit from AI. The strategy for China is obvious: commoditize your complements. Note that Xi explicitly ties openness to AI “moving from the digital world into the physical world”; the physical world is the world dominated by China, and the country’s lead in areas like robotics is going to massively benefit from widely available AI models. Along the same lines, China does not want the U.S. to gain an asymmetric advantage in AI; to the extent that China can weaken the U.S. frontier labs while strengthening any and all potential U.S. adversaries so much the better, and it can benefit from the innovation that will attach itself to an open ecosystem. The Distillation Question By the same token, don’t expect China to do anything about distillation attacks on the frontier labs. I think it is mistaken to attribute all of the success of Chinese labs to distillation, but it’s just as much of a mistake to pretend like distillation doesn’t give Chinese labs a big advantage. That advantage has really come to bear in the last year as post-training reinforcement learning has become increasingly crucial to model performance. Instead of having to fashion reinforcement learning environments from scratch, Chinese labs can simply use frontier labs models as teachers, allowing for rapid improvement at much lower costs (this is not the only reason why Chinese models are cheaper to develop, but it’s a big one). What is interesting is that one of the most important use cases for Chinese models in the West is itself distillation. Thinking Machines, for example, which just released an open-weight model, relies on Chinese models to solve the cold start problem for reinforcement learning. Dean Meyer and Konstantine Buhler wrote an excellent article on X explaining that distillation means that Western open weight models are fundamentally disadvantaged relative to China: Distillation does not explain China’s entire open-model lead. Chinese labs have world-class researchers, substantial compute, strong pre-trained models, software-hardware codesign, and rapidly improving post-training capabilities. But distillation compresses the costly final gap between a strong base and a near-frontier system. Even if distillation represents a smaller share of a Chinese model’s total capability, it represents a meaningful share of its advantage over American open models. New enforcement mechanisms will make large-scale distillation harder, slower, and more expensive for Chinese companies. However, enforcement will not eliminate distillation backed by state actors. Every Western frontier advance therefore creates another teacher for Chinese labs. Western builders must either reproduce those capabilities independently or wait to learn from Chinese models. This gap gives Chinese labs a recurring structural advantage over Western companies. This is a point that bears repeating: because U.S. open weight model makers must follow the frontier labs’ terms of service, they (1) are worse than Chinese alternatives and (2) end up distilling the distillation, just with a detour through Chinese labs. Wouldn’t it be better if western open weight model makers could go to the source? To that end, here’s an even more interesting question around distillation: why exactly is it bad? After all, what are large language models but the distillation of all of the knowledge on the open Internet, scraped by the frontier labs and distilled into the models that are themselves being distilled? Who is exactly being wronged here? In fact, this paradox is the solution. I believe that open weight models are good for innovation (and, per the above, I think that labs on the frontier will be fine), but it’s a problem to be dependent on China. The U.S. should pass a law that (1) makes explicit that collecting data for training models is fair use, and (2) bars terms of service that forbid distillation, for U.S. companies at a minimum. Stopping distillation — which is literally just querying the API — is nearly impossible; the U.S. should go the other way and lean into a new copyright policy that both indemnifies the labs and also guarantees that what they learned fuels further innovation for everyone else. The Reason to Be Afraid This entire Article has been an exercise in defusing overreaction to Kimi K3 specifically and Chinese open weight models generally; however, there is one reason to be concerned, and that is cybersecurity. Consider this story from The Stack: Hugging Face said its production infrastructure was breached by an “autonomous” AI agent system early last week. The platform’s security team were initially stymied in their incident response (IR) by unnamed US LLM frontier model guardrails “which cannot distinguish an incident responder from an attacker,” they said. So Hugging Face’s defenders turned instead to the open-source GLM 5.2 model from China’s Z.ai lab – running it on their own infrastructure to analyse the 17,000+ logs, or footprints, that the attackers left behind. That’s a striking public admission for the New York-headquartered Hugging Face, which lets users collaborate on models, datasets and applications, and which this summer hit the $100 million ARR mark. In an incident report, the company recommended that defenders “have a capable model you can run on your own infrastructure [our italics] vetted and ready before an incident, both to avoid guardrail lockout and to keep attacker data and credentials from leaving your environment.” It’s difficult to overstate how wrong-headed the Trump administration’s panicked response to Anthropic’s release of Fable was, particularly since it exacerbated Anthropic’s worst tendencies in terms of assuming only they can be trusted with powerful AI. In a world with only one AI, it might make sense to reserve the most powerful cybersecurity capabilities for the U.S. government and trusted allies; however, that’s not the world we live in. There are and will be models eminently capable of mounting cybersecurity attacks on existing infrastructure, and those models will be — already are — widely available. The best defense — the only viable defense, in fact — will be to make sure defenders have access to the best models as well. Right now defenders are effectively banned from using Fable or Sol for cybersecurity because of Trump administration directives; that means the best alternative is using models from a country which has been trying to weaken our cyber defenses for years. This is insane! The better course is clear: first, loosen Fable and Sol restrictions on cybersecurity, and second, ensure that U.S. open weight model makers are on an equal playing field with China. Yes, the frontier labs will kick and scream about this, but the Administration should realize that listening to their histrionics has led the U.S. to a position where U.S. companies are dependent on China for their defenses. Let the frontier labs win by being better; don’t let them define safety or security, or pull up the ladder of humanity’s collective knowledge. China is already hard enough to compete with; letting them carry the standard for openness and innovation is simply giving away our biggest advantage.

blocks.html_en: There’s a story I tell about my first day in STRT-431 at Kellogg School of Management, the introductory class that every first-year MBA was required to take; I leafed through the readings and case studies and was dismayed that there weren’t any tech companies on the docket. Me being me, I spoke to the professor after class wondering why, and was told that the goal of the course was not to necessarily learn about specific industries, but rather to uncover broadly applicable universal principles that could be applied to any company in any industry.

sentences.en: There’s a story I tell about my first day in STRT-431 at Kellogg School of Management, the introductory class that every first-year MBA was required to take; I leafed through the readings and case studies and was dismayed that there weren’t any tech companies on the docket.
sentences.zh: 我讲了一个关于我在凯洛格管理学院参加 STRT-431 第一天的故事，这是每个 MBA 一年级学生都必须参加的入门课程；我翻阅了阅读材料和案例研究，但令人沮丧的是，名单上没有任何科技公司。

sentences.en: Me being me, I spoke to the professor after class wondering why, and was told that the goal of the course was not to necessarily learn about specific industries, but rather to uncover broadly applicable universal principles that could be applied to any company in any industry.
sentences.zh: 就我而言，我在课后与教授交谈，想知道为什么，并被告知该课程的目标不一定是了解特定行业，而是发现广泛适用的通用原则，可以应用于任何行业的任何公司。

blocks.html_en: I did not, as I usually tell the story, find this very satisfactory: to me the nature of tech, particularly the fact that software and distribution had zero marginal costs (and zero transaction costs), was something fundamentally different; putting in zeroes in formulas tends to wreak havoc! I soon realized, however, that that was my opportunity. The fundamental insight undergirding Aggregation Theory is that zero marginal costs leads to fundamentally different value chains than people once expected from the Internet: centralization and scale in a world where controlling demand mattered more than distributing supply.

sentences.en: I did not, as I usually tell the story, find this very satisfactory: to me the nature of tech, particularly the fact that software and distribution had zero marginal costs (and zero transaction costs), was something fundamentally different; putting in zeroes in formulas tends to wreak havoc!
sentences.zh: 正如我通常讲述的那样，我并不觉得这非常令人满意：对我来说，技术的本质，特别是软件和发行的边际成本为零（以及零交易成本）的事实，是根本不同的；在公式中输入零往往会造成严重破坏！

sentences.en: I soon realized, however, that that was my opportunity.
sentences.zh: 然而，我很快意识到，这是我的机会。

sentences.en: The fundamental insight undergirding Aggregation Theory is that zero marginal costs leads to fundamentally different value chains than people once expected from the Internet: centralization and scale in a world where controlling demand mattered more than distributing supply.
sentences.zh: 支持聚合理论的基本观点是，零边际成本导致的价值链与人们曾经对互联网的预期完全不同：在一个控制需求比分配供应更重要的世界中，集中化和规模化。

blocks.html_en: What is fascinating about AI, however, is the extent to which those old universal principles are coming back to the forefront. That was never more apparent than this past weekend, when arguments raged on X about the implications of Kimi K3, another open weights model out of China, approaching the state-of-the-art in terms of capabilities. The long and short of it is this: marginal costs are back in a big way, both in terms of short-term implications of state-of-the-art free models, and in terms of the long-term structure of the industry.

sentences.en: What is fascinating about AI, however, is the extent to which those old universal principles are coming back to the forefront.
sentences.zh: 然而，人工智能的迷人之处在于，这些旧的普遍原则在多大程度上重新回到了人们的关注焦点。

sentences.en: That was never more apparent than this past weekend, when arguments raged on X about the implications of Kimi K3, another open weights model out of China, approaching the state-of-the-art in terms of capabilities.
sentences.zh: 这一点在上周末表现得最为明显，当时 X 上关于 Kimi K3 的影响的争论非常激烈，Kimi K3 是中国的另一个开放权重模型，在功能方面接近最先进的模型。

sentences.en: The long and short of it is this: marginal costs are back in a big way, both in terms of short-term implications of state-of-the-art free models, and in terms of the long-term structure of the industry.
sentences.zh: 总而言之，边际成本大幅回归，无论是从最先进的免费模式的短期影响来看，还是从行业的长期结构来看。

blocks.html_en: COGS Versus R&D

sentences.en: COGS Versus R&D
sentences.zh: 销货成本与研发

blocks.html_en: One of the most common misconceptions undergirding discussion of open weights models is that they are cheaper — free, even. After all, you can just download the weights, and skip the time and expense and capabilities necessary to create your own model. That is, of course, true, but the “free” in this case is a reference to the amount you need to spend on research and development; R&D is a fixed expense that is independent of the revenue you generate. If you spend $1 million in R&D, it doesn’t matter if you do $100 thousand in revenue or $100 million; you still spent $1 million on R&D (it does, of course, impact your profitability).

sentences.en: One of the most common misconceptions undergirding discussion of open weights models is that they are cheaper — free, even.
sentences.zh: 讨论开放权重模型时最常见的误解之一是它们更便宜，甚至免费。

sentences.en: After all, you can just download the weights, and skip the time and expense and capabilities necessary to create your own model.
sentences.zh: 毕竟，您只需下载权重，即可跳过创建自己的模型所需的时间、费用和功能。

sentences.en: That is, of course, true, but the “free” in this case is a reference to the amount you need to spend on research and development; R&D is a fixed expense that is independent of the revenue you generate.
sentences.zh: 当然，这是事实，但这里的“免费”是指你需要在研发上花费的金额；研发是一项固定费用，独立于您产生的收入。

sentences.en: If you spend $1 million in R&D, it doesn’t matter if you do $100 thousand in revenue or $100 million; you still spent $1 million on R&D (it does, of course, impact your profitability).
sentences.zh: 如果你在研发上花费 100 万美元，那么你的收入是 10 万美元还是 1 亿美元都没有关系；您仍然在研发上花费了 100 万美元（当然，这确实会影响您的盈利能力）。

blocks.html_en: What is related to revenue is COGS — cost of goods sold — and COGS is real for AI in a way it hasn’t been for software for a very long time. Specifically, running inference on a model — whether that model be Kimi or Fable — costs money, and the amount of money an AI provider spends on inference is, at least in most business models, directly correlated to revenue. To reuse the above example, generating $100 million versus $100 thousand in revenue will likely require 1,000x COGS. In concrete terms, if it costs 50 cents to generate the tokens that drive $1 in revenue, then $100 million in revenue will have $50 million in COGS; $100 thousand in revenue will only have $50 thousand in COGS.

sentences.en: What is related to revenue is COGS — cost of goods sold — and COGS is real for AI in a way it hasn’t been for software for a very long time.
sentences.zh: 与收入相关的是 COGS（销售成本），而 COGS 对于人工智能来说是真实的，而对于软件来说，这种情况已经很长时间了。

sentences.en: Specifically, running inference on a model — whether that model be Kimi or Fable — costs money, and the amount of money an AI provider spends on inference is, at least in most business models, directly correlated to revenue.
sentences.zh: 具体来说，在模型上运行推理（无论该模型是 Kimi 还是 Fable）都需要花钱，而人工智能提供商在推理上花费的金额至少在大多数商业模式中与收入直接相关。

sentences.en: To reuse the above example, generating $100 million versus $100 thousand in revenue will likely require 1,000x COGS.
sentences.zh: 要重复使用上面的示例，产生 1 亿美元与 10 万美元的收入可能需要 1,000 倍的销货成本。

sentences.en: In concrete terms, if it costs 50 cents to generate the tokens that drive $1 in revenue, then $100 million in revenue will have $50 million in COGS; $100 thousand in revenue will only have $50 thousand in COGS.
sentences.zh: 具体而言，如果生成可带来 1 美元收入的代币的成本为 50 美分，那么 1 亿美元的收入将有 5000 万美元的 COGS； 10 万美元的收入只有 5 万美元的销货成本。

blocks.html_en: The point in terms of open weight models is that they are not free to serve. Kimi K3 costs $3 per million input tokens, and $15 per million output tokens; that is cheaper than Sol’s $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, but that might not even be the right measurement.

sentences.en: The point in terms of open weight models is that they are not free to serve.
sentences.zh: 开放权重模型的要点是它们不是免费提供的。

sentences.en: Kimi K3 costs $3 per million input tokens, and $15 per million output tokens; that is cheaper than Sol’s $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens, but that might not even be the right measurement.
sentences.zh: Kimi K3 每百万输入代币的成本为 3 美元，每百万输出代币的成本为 15 美元；这比 Sol 的每百万输入代币 5 美元和每百万输出代币 30 美元便宜，但这甚至可能不是正确的衡量标准。

blocks.html_en: Tokens Versus Intelligence

sentences.en: Tokens Versus Intelligence
sentences.zh: 代币与情报

blocks.html_en: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has described what Nvidia is building as “token factories”, and from Nvidia’s perspective that framing makes sense. Nvidia GPUs are model agnostic: they generate tokens, and do so in the fastest and most efficient way possible. That leads to measurements like tokens-per-second, time-to-first-token, tokens-per-watt, token cost, etc., and Huang argues that these metrics will be the basis for decision-making.

sentences.en: Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang has described what Nvidia is building as “token factories”, and from Nvidia’s perspective that framing makes sense.
sentences.zh: Nvidia 首席执行官黄仁勋将 Nvidia 正在建设的东西描述为“代币工厂”，从 Nvidia 的角度来看，这种框架是有道理的。

sentences.en: Nvidia GPUs are model agnostic: they generate tokens, and do so in the fastest and most efficient way possible.
sentences.zh: Nvidia GPU 与模型无关：它们生成令牌，并以尽可能最快、最有效的方式生成令牌。

sentences.en: That leads to measurements like tokens-per-second, time-to-first-token, tokens-per-watt, token cost, etc., and Huang argues that these metrics will be the basis for decision-making.
sentences.zh: 这导致了诸如每秒令牌数、首次令牌时间、每瓦令牌数、令牌成本等测量，黄认为这些指标将成为决策的基础。

blocks.html_en: This is a framing that definitely made sense during the first paradigm of AI, the ChatGPT era, when tokens were delivered straight to the end user. The second paradigm of AI, however, the reasoning era, confounds this measurement. Reasoning entails an explosion in chain-of-thought tokens, and different models need different amounts of reasoning tokens to arrive at the right answer. Kimi, for example, reportedly uses significantly more tokens than Sol, rendering its price advantage moot. Agents introduce a similar dynamic: some models are more efficient than others in terms of the number of tokens they need to execute agentic workflows.

sentences.en: This is a framing that definitely made sense during the first paradigm of AI, the ChatGPT era, when tokens were delivered straight to the end user.
sentences.zh: 这个框架在人工智能的第一个范式（ChatGPT 时代）中绝对有意义，当时代币直接交付给最终用户。

sentences.en: The second paradigm of AI, however, the reasoning era, confounds this measurement.
sentences.zh: 然而，人工智能的第二个范式，即推理时代，混淆了这种衡量标准。

sentences.en: Reasoning entails an explosion in chain-of-thought tokens, and different models need different amounts of reasoning tokens to arrive at the right answer.
sentences.zh: 推理需要思维链令牌的爆炸式增长，不同的模型需要不同数量的推理令牌才能得出正确的答案。

sentences.en: Kimi, for example, reportedly uses significantly more tokens than Sol, rendering its price advantage moot.
sentences.zh: 例如，据报道 Kimi 使用的代币远多于 Sol，使其价格优势变得毫无意义。

sentences.en: Agents introduce a similar dynamic: some models are more efficient than others in terms of the number of tokens they need to execute agentic workflows.
sentences.zh: 代理引入了类似的动态：就执行代理工作流程所需的令牌数量而言，某些模型比其他模型更有效。

blocks.html_en: What this means is that tokens are not a commodity. The defining characteristic of a commodity is that it is fungible: a gallon of oil is a gallon of oil; a ton of copper is a ton of copper; a bushel of wheat is a bushel of wheat. A token from one model, however, is not the same as a token from another model. What is fungible is what is constructed from tokens, which is to say intelligence. In other words, if both Kimi and Sol generated the right answer, then that answer is fungible; the difference in tokens generated to get to that right answer is a contributor to a difference in COGS.

sentences.en: What this means is that tokens are not a commodity.
sentences.zh: 这意味着代币不是商品。

sentences.en: The defining characteristic of a commodity is that it is fungible: a gallon of oil is a gallon of oil; a ton of copper is a ton of copper; a bushel of wheat is a bushel of wheat.
sentences.zh: 商品的定义特征是它是可替代的：一加仑石油就是一加仑石油；一吨铜就是一吨铜；一蒲式耳小麦就是一蒲式耳小麦。

sentences.en: A token from one model, however, is not the same as a token from another model.
sentences.zh: 然而，一种模型的令牌与另一种模型的令牌不同。

sentences.en: What is fungible is what is constructed from tokens, which is to say intelligence.
sentences.zh: 可替代的是由代币构建的东西，即智能。

sentences.en: In other words, if both Kimi and Sol generated the right answer, then that answer is fungible; the difference in tokens generated to get to that right answer is a contributor to a difference in COGS.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，如果 Kimi 和 Sol 都生成了正确的答案，那么该答案是可替代的；为获得正确答案而生成的代币差异是导致 COGS 差异的一个因素。

blocks.html_en: The COGS for intelligence is a function of a few different factors:

sentences.en: The COGS for intelligence is a function of a few different factors:
sentences.zh: 智力的 COGS 是几个不同因素的函数：

blocks.html_en: • Model footprint: The weights and runtime state determine how much expensive memory and how many accelerators are required to host each serving replica.

sentences.en: Model footprint: The weights and runtime state determine how much expensive memory and how many accelerators are required to host each serving replica.
sentences.zh: 模型占用空间：权重和运行时状态决定了托管每个服务副本需要多少昂贵的内存和多少个加速器。

blocks.html_en: • Inference efficiency: Architectural choices (e.g. Mixture-of-Experts) reduce computation per generated token.

sentences.en: Inference efficiency: Architectural choices (e.g. Mixture-of-Experts) reduce computation per generated token.
sentences.zh: 推理效率：架构选择（例如专家混合）减少了每个生成令牌的计算量。

blocks.html_en: • Memory efficiency: Architectural choices can reduce KV cache requirements, allowing more concurrent requests and better GPU utilization.

sentences.en: Memory efficiency: Architectural choices can reduce KV cache requirements, allowing more concurrent requests and better GPU utilization.
sentences.zh: 内存效率：架构选择可以减少 KV 缓存需求，从而允许更多并发请求和更好的 GPU 利用率。

blocks.html_en: • Serving efficiency: Batching, scheduling, prefix caching, and other inference optimizations maximize utilization and share work across requests.

sentences.en: Serving efficiency: Batching, scheduling, prefix caching, and other inference optimizations maximize utilization and share work across requests.
sentences.zh: 服务效率：批处理、调度、前缀缓存和其他推理优化可最大限度地提高利用率并跨请求共享工作。

blocks.html_en: • Token efficiency: The fewer tokens required to reach a correct answer, the lower the inference cost.

sentences.en: Token efficiency: The fewer tokens required to reach a correct answer, the lower the inference cost.
sentences.zh: 令牌效率：得出正确答案所需的令牌越少，推理成本就越低。

blocks.html_en: The reason this matters is that we are rapidly approaching a state in which intelligence for many economically beneficial tasks is in fact a commodity. Anyone building a basic CRUD app, for example, can likely do so using models from multiple providers. And, in a commodity market, the route to profitability is not through charging higher prices — again, you can (or will soon be able to) make the exact same app using multiple models — but rather through having a superior cost structure.

sentences.en: The reason this matters is that we are rapidly approaching a state in which intelligence for many economically beneficial tasks is in fact a commodity.
sentences.zh: 这很重要的原因是，我们正在迅速接近这样一种状态，在这种状态下，许多经济上有益的任务的智能实际上是一种商品。

sentences.en: Anyone building a basic CRUD app, for example, can likely do so using models from multiple providers.
sentences.zh: 例如，任何构建基本 CRUD 应用程序的人都可以使用来自多个提供商的模型来实现这一点。

sentences.en: And, in a commodity market, the route to profitability is not through charging higher prices — again, you can (or will soon be able to) make the exact same app using multiple models — but rather through having a superior cost structure.
sentences.zh: 而且，在商品市场中，盈利的途径不是通过收取更高的价格——同样，你可以（或很快就能）使用多种模型制作完全相同的应用程序——而是通过拥有优越的成本结构。

blocks.html_en: Understanding Commodity Markets

sentences.en: Understanding Commodity Markets
sentences.zh: 了解商品市场

blocks.html_en: It’s worth stepping through the mechanics here, because, as I noted a few months ago in Amazon’s Durability, the dynamics of commodity markets are not something people in tech are generally familiar with:

sentences.en: It’s worth stepping through the mechanics here, because, as I noted a few months ago in Amazon’s Durability, the dynamics of commodity markets are not something people in tech are generally familiar with:
sentences.zh: 值得逐步了解这里的机制，因为正如我几个月前在亚马逊的耐用性中指出的那样，大宗商品市场的动态并不是科技界人士普遍熟悉的：

blocks.html_en: • In commodity markets, everyone charges the same price, because everyone is selling the same thing; that price is determined by supply and demand.

sentences.en: In commodity markets, everyone charges the same price, because everyone is selling the same thing; that price is determined by supply and demand.
sentences.zh: 在商品市场上，每个人都收取相同的价格，因为每个人都在销售相同的东西；该价格是由供需决定的。

blocks.html_en: • The demand for a commodity is a function of price elasticity: the cheaper the commodity, the more demand there is for it, and vice-versa.

sentences.en: The demand for a commodity is a function of price elasticity: the cheaper the commodity, the more demand there is for it, and vice-versa.
sentences.zh: 对商品的需求是价格弹性的函数：商品越便宜，需求就越大，反之亦然。

blocks.html_en: • The supply for a commodity is a function of the marginal cost of producing the commodity.

sentences.en: The supply for a commodity is a function of the marginal cost of producing the commodity.
sentences.zh: 商品的供给是生产该商品的边际成本的函数。

blocks.html_en: The key thing to understand is that the marginal cost of producing the commodity differs by supplier. What this means in practice is that the supplier with the worst cost structure ends up selling the commodity at their marginal cost (if they can produce at all); the profits of everyone else depend on the extent to which their cost structure is better than the marginal supplier.

sentences.en: The key thing to understand is that the marginal cost of producing the commodity differs by supplier.
sentences.zh: 需要理解的关键是，生产商品的边际成本因供应商而异。

sentences.en: What this means in practice is that the supplier with the worst cost structure ends up selling the commodity at their marginal cost (if they can produce at all); the profits of everyone else depend on the extent to which their cost structure is better than the marginal supplier.
sentences.zh: 这在实践中意味着成本结构最差的供应商最终会以其边际成本出售商品（如果他们能够生产的话）；其他所有人的利润取决于他们的成本结构优于边际供应商的程度。

blocks.html_en: As an example:

sentences.en: As an example:
sentences.zh: 举个例子：

blocks.html_en: • Supplier A can produce 10 units of the commodity for $10 each

sentences.en: Supplier A can produce 10 units of the commodity for $10 each
sentences.zh: 供应商 A 可以生产 10 单位该商品，每单位 10 美元

blocks.html_en: • Supplier B can produce 10 units of the commodity for $15 each

sentences.en: Supplier B can produce 10 units of the commodity for $15 each
sentences.zh: 供应商 B 可以生产 10 单位该商品，每单位 15 美元

blocks.html_en: • Supplier C can produce 10 units of the commodity for $20 each

sentences.en: Supplier C can produce 10 units of the commodity for $20 each
sentences.zh: 供应商 C 可以生产 10 单位该商品，每单位 20 美元

blocks.html_en: Let’s assume the price elasticity is such that there is demand for 25 units of the commodity at $20. That means:

sentences.en: Let’s assume the price elasticity is such that there is demand for 25 units of the commodity at $20.
sentences.zh: 假设价格弹性为 20 美元，对 25 单位商品的需求。

sentences.en: That means:
sentences.zh: 这意味着：

blocks.html_en: • Supplier A will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $10/unit

sentences.en: Supplier A will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $10/unit
sentences.zh: 供应商 A 将以 20 美元的价格出售 10 单位商品，每单位赚取 10 美元

blocks.html_en: • Supplier B will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $5/unit

sentences.en: Supplier B will sell 10 units of the commodity for $20, earning $5/unit
sentences.zh: 供应商 B 将以 20 美元的价格出售 10 单位的商品，每单位赚取 5 美元

blocks.html_en: • Supplier C will sell 5 units of the commodity for $20, earning $0/unit

sentences.en: Supplier C will sell 5 units of the commodity for $20, earning $0/unit
sentences.zh: 供应商 C 将以 20 美元的价格出售 5 单位商品，每单位赚取 0 美元

blocks.html_en: This isn’t precisely right: the reason why Supplier C will bear the shortfall is because Suppliers A and B will be able to slightly undercut them in price, which will of course affect demand (which is elastic), but it makes the point. Supplier A has a great business, Supplier B has a good business, and Supplier C is going to go bankrupt.

sentences.en: This isn’t precisely right: the reason why Supplier C will bear the shortfall is because Suppliers A and B will be able to slightly undercut them in price, which will of course affect demand (which is elastic), but it makes the point.
sentences.zh: 这并不完全正确：供应商 C 之所以要承担缺口，是因为供应商 A 和 B 能够稍微压低他们的价格，这当然会影响需求（有弹性），但它说明了这一点。

sentences.en: Supplier A has a great business, Supplier B has a good business, and Supplier C is going to go bankrupt.
sentences.zh: 供应商A生意很好，供应商B生意很好，而供应商C就要破产了。

blocks.html_en: Bankruptcy risk is where fixed costs come back to the forefront: Supplier C has both fixed costs (like potentially R&D spend) and also may have taken on debt to finance the equipment necessary to produce the commodity. It can’t price its commodity with these costs in mind — remember, the market-clearing price approximates the marginal cost of the highest-cost unit needed to satisfy demand — but those costs can absolutely drive the supplier out of business. And, if that supplier goes out of business, then prices go up, until another supplier decides to enter (or the other suppliers expand).

sentences.en: Bankruptcy risk is where fixed costs come back to the forefront: Supplier C has both fixed costs (like potentially R&D spend) and also may have taken on debt to finance the equipment necessary to produce the commodity.
sentences.zh: 破产风险是固定成本再次成为首要问题的地方：供应商 C 既有固定成本（如潜在的研发支出），也可能承担债务来为生产商品所需的设备提供资金。

sentences.en: It can’t price its commodity with these costs in mind — remember, the market-clearing price approximates the marginal cost of the highest-cost unit needed to satisfy demand — but those costs can absolutely drive the supplier out of business.
sentences.zh: 它不能在考虑这些成本的情况下为其商品定价——记住，市场出清价格近似于满足需求所需的最高成本单位的边际成本——但这些成本绝对会让供应商破产。

sentences.en: And, if that supplier goes out of business, then prices go up, until another supplier decides to enter (or the other suppliers expand).
sentences.zh: 而且，如果该供应商倒闭，那么价格就会上涨，直到另一个供应商决定进入（或其他供应商扩张）。

blocks.html_en: The Intelligence Market

sentences.en: The Intelligence Market
sentences.zh: 情报市场

blocks.html_en: Let’s bring this back to models. Right now, none of the above analysis applies because demand exceeds supply for frontier models, and supply is limited by a lack of compute. This compute shortage doesn’t just mean that a compute supplier like Nvidia makes very large margins, but also that Nvidia’s customers, like SpaceXAI, can turn around and resell compute at high margins as well to a company like Anthropic. Anthropic, meanwhile, can pay the markup because they can sell tokens with a higher markup still.

sentences.en: Let’s bring this back to models.
sentences.zh: 让我们把它带回到模型上。

sentences.en: Right now, none of the above analysis applies because demand exceeds supply for frontier models, and supply is limited by a lack of compute.
sentences.zh: 目前，上述分析均不适用，因为前沿模型的需求超过了供应，而供应又因缺乏计算而受到限制。

sentences.en: This compute shortage doesn’t just mean that a compute supplier like Nvidia makes very large margins, but also that Nvidia’s customers, like SpaceXAI, can turn around and resell compute at high margins as well to a company like Anthropic.
sentences.zh: 这种计算短缺不仅意味着像 Nvidia 这样的计算供应商可以获得非常高的利润，而且还意味着 Nvidia 的客户，比如 SpaceXAI，也可以以高利润转售计算给像 Anthropic 这样的公司。

sentences.en: Anthropic, meanwhile, can pay the markup because they can sell tokens with a higher markup still.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，Anthropic 可以支付加价，因为他们可以以更高的加价出售代币。

blocks.html_en: It’s not just excess demand that gives Anthropic great margins, however: Anthropic and OpenAI likely have among the lowest costs per unit of frontier-quality intelligence, thanks to model capability, serving scale, and token efficiency. They are serving models at a particular capability level for months before their competitors, and are simultaneously applying the best models to optimizing those costs.

sentences.en: It’s not just excess demand that gives Anthropic great margins, however: Anthropic and OpenAI likely have among the lowest costs per unit of frontier-quality intelligence, thanks to model capability, serving scale, and token efficiency.
sentences.zh: 然而，给 Anthropic 带来巨大利润的不仅仅是过剩的需求：得益于模型能力、服务规模和代币效率，Anthropic 和 OpenAI 可能是单位前沿质量智能成本最低的公司之一。

sentences.en: They are serving models at a particular capability level for months before their competitors, and are simultaneously applying the best models to optimizing those costs.
sentences.zh: 他们比竞争对手早几个月以特定的能力水平提供模型，并同时应用最佳模型来优化这些成本。

blocks.html_en: It’s also worth noting that the market is not yet treating intelligence like a commodity: demand is for Anthropic and OpenAI specifically, and much less for models that aren’t as good (thus SpaceXAI and Meta selling capacity to Anthropic); one way to think about the push for optimizing cost is that that is a function of defining jobs-to-be-done by intelligence level, such that intelligence buyers can create a market where intelligence is commoditized. In the long run, however, whoever is on the frontier is the best placed to dominate non-frontier markets as well, which are just the frontier minus n-months, i.e. months in which the frontier model makers have been optimizing their cost of serving.

sentences.en: It’s also worth noting that the market is not yet treating intelligence like a commodity: demand is for Anthropic and OpenAI specifically, and much less for models that aren’t as good (thus SpaceXAI and Meta selling capacity to Anthropic); one way to think about the push for optimizing cost is that that is a function of defining jobs-to-be-done by intelligence level, such that intelligence buyers can create a market where intelligence is commoditized.
sentences.zh: 还值得注意的是，市场尚未将智能视为商品：需求主要针对 Anthropic 和 OpenAI，更不用说对不太好的模型了（因此 SpaceXAI 和 Meta 向 Anthropic 出售能力）；考虑推动优化成本的一种方法是，这是根据智力水平定义待完成工作的函数，这样智力购买者就可以创建一个智力商品化的市场。

sentences.en: In the long run, however, whoever is on the frontier is the best placed to dominate non-frontier markets as well, which are just the frontier minus n-months, i.e. months in which the frontier model makers have been optimizing their cost of serving.
sentences.zh: 然而，从长远来看，无论谁处于前沿，也最有可能主导非前沿市场，非前沿市场只是前沿减去n个月，即前沿模型制造商一直在优化其服务成本的几个月。

blocks.html_en: All of this is to say that I think the reaction to Kimi and Chinese models generally is pretty over-blown, at least from an economic perspective. Right now there is a price umbrella that is downstream of the lack of compute; I highly doubt that Chinese models are cheaper to serve on a marginal cost basis, they just seem cheaper because Anthropic and OpenAI are so supply constrained that they are charging far more than they would if there were sufficient supply to meet the demand for intelligence.

sentences.en: All of this is to say that I think the reaction to Kimi and Chinese models generally is pretty over-blown, at least from an economic perspective.
sentences.zh: 所有这些都表明，我认为人们对《基米》和中国模式的反应总体上是相当夸大的，至少从经济角度来看是这样。

sentences.en: Right now there is a price umbrella that is downstream of the lack of compute; I highly doubt that Chinese models are cheaper to serve on a marginal cost basis, they just seem cheaper because Anthropic and OpenAI are so supply constrained that they are charging far more than they would if there were sufficient supply to meet the demand for intelligence.
sentences.zh: 目前，计算能力不足的下游存在一个价格保护伞；我非常怀疑中国模型的边际成本是否更便宜，它们只是看起来更便宜，因为 Anthropic 和 OpenAI 的供应非常有限，以至于它们的收费远远高于有足够供应来满足情报需求的情况。

blocks.html_en: Frontier Lab Paranoia

sentences.en: Frontier Lab Paranoia
sentences.zh: 前沿实验室偏执狂

blocks.html_en: Why, then, do the model makers in particular seem so panicked about Chinese models?

sentences.en: Why, then, do the model makers in particular seem so panicked about Chinese models?
sentences.zh: 那么，为什么模型制造商尤其对中国模型如此恐慌呢？

blocks.html_en: First, I think the frontier labs are anchored in a world where training costs dominated their financial modeling. As long as training consumed more GPUs than inference, it was critical to maximize inference revenue to help fund the next training run, which meant charging very high prices for inference.

sentences.en: First, I think the frontier labs are anchored in a world where training costs dominated their financial modeling.
sentences.zh: 首先，我认为前沿实验室立足于一个培训成本主导其财务模型的世界。

sentences.en: As long as training consumed more GPUs than inference, it was critical to maximize inference revenue to help fund the next training run, which meant charging very high prices for inference.
sentences.zh: 只要训练消耗的 GPU 多于推理，就必须最大限度地提高推理收入，为下一次训练运行提供资金，这意味着要对推理收取非常高的价格。

blocks.html_en: Going forward, however, I expect the inference market to grow much faster than training costs (and that includes the assumption that training costs will continue to skyrocket), which means they really can make it up in volume. It wasn’t clear this would be the case as recently as eight months ago, but the agent paradigm unlock is so massive that frontier labs should have more confidence that they can not just survive but thrive with lower prices (once they have sufficient compute).

sentences.en: Going forward, however, I expect the inference market to grow much faster than training costs (and that includes the assumption that training costs will continue to skyrocket), which means they really can make it up in volume.
sentences.zh: 然而，展望未来，我预计推理市场的增长速度将远远快于培训成本（其中包括培训成本将继续飙升的假设），这意味着它们确实可以通过数量来弥补。

sentences.en: It wasn’t clear this would be the case as recently as eight months ago, but the agent paradigm unlock is so massive that frontier labs should have more confidence that they can not just survive but thrive with lower prices (once they have sufficient compute).
sentences.zh: 八个月前还不清楚情况是否会如此，但代理范式的解锁是如此巨大，以至于前沿实验室应该更有信心，他们不仅能够生存，而且能够以较低的价格蓬勃发展（一旦他们拥有足够的计算能力）。

blocks.html_en: Second, intelligence isn’t in fact a perfect commodity, in part because applied intelligence makes itself smarter. Specifically, whoever is running inference is also collecting data, and that data goes into making the next iteration of the model better. This is, on one hand, all the more reason for the frontier labs to lower prices and increase usage as more compute comes online; on the other hand, this is why companies like Microsoft are increasingly obsessed with helping companies run their own models. That is much more viable if Chinese models are a viable alternative.

sentences.en: Second, intelligence isn’t in fact a perfect commodity, in part because applied intelligence makes itself smarter.
sentences.zh: 其次，智能实际上并不是一种完美的商品，部分原因是应用智能使自身变得更加聪明。

sentences.en: Specifically, whoever is running inference is also collecting data, and that data goes into making the next iteration of the model better.
sentences.zh: 具体来说，运行推理的人也在收集数据，并且这些数据可以使模型的下一次迭代变得更好。

sentences.en: This is, on one hand, all the more reason for the frontier labs to lower prices and increase usage as more compute comes online; on the other hand, this is why companies like Microsoft are increasingly obsessed with helping companies run their own models.
sentences.zh: 一方面，随着更多计算的上线，前沿实验室更有理由降低价格并增加使用量；另一方面，这就是为什么像微软这样的公司越来越热衷于帮助企业运行自己的模型。

sentences.en: That is much more viable if Chinese models are a viable alternative.
sentences.zh: 如果中国模式是可行的替代方案，那就更可行。

blocks.html_en: Third, the other way that frontier labs can not only differentiate from Chinese models but also from each other is by continuing to integrate up into the customer experience. It’s striking the extent to which Claude Code and Codex are proving to be quite sticky; whichever harness you start working with is likely to be the one you stick with, and that figures to be even more the case with non-technical users. And, in the long run, this imperative to move up the stack does mean that frontier models are absolutely a threat to software providers, including Microsoft. On the flipside, the extent to which software companies who currently own the customer experience have access to competitive models is the extent to which they may be able to resist the encroachment of the frontier labs.

sentences.en: Third, the other way that frontier labs can not only differentiate from Chinese models but also from each other is by continuing to integrate up into the customer experience.
sentences.zh: 第三，前沿实验室不仅可以区别于中国模式，还可以区别于其他实验室的另一种方式是继续融入客户体验。

sentences.en: It’s striking the extent to which Claude Code and Codex are proving to be quite sticky; whichever harness you start working with is likely to be the one you stick with, and that figures to be even more the case with non-technical users.
sentences.zh: 事实证明，克劳德法典和法典的粘性程度令人震惊。无论您开始使用哪一种工具，您都可能会坚持使用，对于非技术用户来说更是如此。

sentences.en: And, in the long run, this imperative to move up the stack does mean that frontier models are absolutely a threat to software providers, including Microsoft.
sentences.zh: 而且，从长远来看，提升堆栈的必要性确实意味着前沿模型绝对对包括微软在内的软件提供商构成威胁。

sentences.en: On the flipside, the extent to which software companies who currently own the customer experience have access to competitive models is the extent to which they may be able to resist the encroachment of the frontier labs.
sentences.zh: 另一方面，目前拥有客户体验的软件公司能够在多大程度上获得竞争模型，就是他们能够在多大程度上抵制前沿实验室的侵蚀。

blocks.html_en: Finally, the ideological angle of Anthropic in particular is impossible to ignore. This is a company that believes only it can be entrusted with AI, and the existence of open weights alternatives strikes a fatal blow to that presumption.

sentences.en: Finally, the ideological angle of Anthropic in particular is impossible to ignore.
sentences.zh: 最后，人择学的意识形态角度尤其不容忽视。

sentences.en: This is a company that believes only it can be entrusted with AI, and the existence of open weights alternatives strikes a fatal blow to that presumption.
sentences.zh: 这是一家相信只有自己才能托付人工智能的公司，而开放权重替代品的存在对这一假设造成了致命的打击。

blocks.html_en: China’s Motivation

sentences.en: China’s Motivation
sentences.zh: 中国的动力

blocks.html_en: Kimi isn’t the only new Chinese model; from Bloomberg:

sentences.en: Kimi isn’t the only new Chinese model; from Bloomberg:
sentences.zh: Kimi并不是唯一的中国新模特；来自彭博社：

blocks.html_en:
Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. shares rose as much as 5.4% on Monday after the company launched a preview version of its flagship Qwen3.8 Max model, describing it as second only to Anthropic PBC’s Fable 5. The Sunday release came only days after startup Moonshot AI unveiled a powerful new offering that’s roiled markets and triggered concern in the US about China closing the gap on global leaders like Anthropic and OpenAI. Qwen3.8 Max has 2.4 trillion parameters, joining Moonshot’s Kimi K3 in the heavyweight class. With 2.8 trillion parameters, K3 rivals top offerings and Alibaba is setting similarly high expectations.

Developers can now access Qwen3.8 Max through Alibaba’s coding platforms, including Qoder. Alibaba plans to make the model open-weight soon, expanding access beyond the preview release. Interest in these made-in-China artificial intelligence systems and models is so high that Moonshot was forced to pause taking on new subscriptions late on Sunday to manage overwhelming demand.

sentences.en: Alibaba plans to make the model open-weight soon, expanding access beyond the preview release.
sentences.zh: 阿里巴巴计划很快开放该模型，将访问范围扩大到预览版之外。

sentences.en: Interest in these made-in-China artificial intelligence systems and models is so high that Moonshot was forced to pause taking on new subscriptions late on Sunday to manage overwhelming demand.
sentences.zh: 人们对这些中国制造的人工智能系统和模型的兴趣如此之高，以至于 Moonshot 被迫在周日晚些时候暂停接受新订阅，以应对巨大的需求。

blocks.html_en: The fact that Qwen3.8 Max will also have open weights is notable. Alibaba stopped releasing weights for its leading edge models earlier this year, but appears to have reverted that change; I suspect that shift was related to last week’s Xi Jinping speech about AI that doubled down on the open weights approach:

sentences.zh: 值得注意的是，Qwen3.8 Max 也将具有开放配重。

sentences.en: Alibaba stopped releasing weights for its leading edge models earlier this year, but appears to have reverted that change; I suspect that shift was related to last week’s Xi Jinping speech about AI that doubled down on the open weights approach:
sentences.zh: 阿里巴巴今年早些时候停止发布其领先模型的权重，但似乎已经恢复了这一变化；我怀疑这种转变与上周习近平关于人工智能的讲话有关，该讲话加倍强调了开放权重方法：

blocks.html_en: We should adhere to the principle of openness and win-win and boost innovation-driven development. As a new engine of world economic growth and an accelerator for the shift of growth drivers, AI is moving from the digital world into the physical world. We should seize this rare, historic opportunity to encourage open source, openness, collaboration and sharing. We should facilitate technological innovation, industrial development and scenario-based application of AI. We should make coordinated advances in the transformation and upgrade of traditional industries, the cultivation and growth of emerging industries and forward-looking planning for future industries, so that all sectors and businesses can benefit from AI.

blocks.html_en: The strategy for China is obvious: commoditize your complements. Note that Xi explicitly ties openness to AI “moving from the digital world into the physical world”; the physical world is the world dominated by China, and the country’s lead in areas like robotics is going to massively benefit from widely available AI models.

sentences.en: The strategy for China is obvious: commoditize your complements.
sentences.zh: 中国的战略很明显：将你的互补品商品化。

sentences.en: Note that Xi explicitly ties openness to AI “moving from the digital world into the physical world”; the physical world is the world dominated by China, and the country’s lead in areas like robotics is going to massively benefit from widely available AI models.
sentences.zh: 请注意，习近平明确将开放性与人工智能“从数字世界转向物理世界”联系起来；现实世界是中国主导的世界，中国在机器人等领域的领先地位将极大地受益于广泛使用的人工智能模型。

blocks.html_en: Along the same lines, China does not want the U.S. to gain an asymmetric advantage in AI; to the extent that China can weaken the U.S. frontier labs while strengthening any and all potential U.S. adversaries so much the better, and it can benefit from the innovation that will attach itself to an open ecosystem.

sentences.en: Along the same lines, China does not want the U.S. to gain an asymmetric advantage in AI; to the extent that China can weaken the U.S. frontier labs while strengthening any and all potential U.S. adversaries so much the better, and it can benefit from the innovation that will attach itself to an open ecosystem.
sentences.zh: 同样，中国也不希望美国在人工智能领域获得不对称优势；在某种程度上，中国可以削弱美国的前沿实验室，同时增强美国所有潜在对手的实力，这样效果就更好，并且它可以从与开放生态系统相结合的创新中受益。

blocks.html_en: The Distillation Question

sentences.en: The Distillation Question
sentences.zh: 蒸馏问题

blocks.html_en: By the same token, don’t expect China to do anything about distillation attacks on the frontier labs. I think it is mistaken to attribute all of the success of Chinese labs to distillation, but it’s just as much of a mistake to pretend like distillation doesn’t give Chinese labs a big advantage. That advantage has really come to bear in the last year as post-training reinforcement learning has become increasingly crucial to model performance. Instead of having to fashion reinforcement learning environments from scratch, Chinese labs can simply use frontier labs models as teachers, allowing for rapid improvement at much lower costs (this is not the only reason why Chinese models are cheaper to develop, but it’s a big one).

sentences.en: By the same token, don’t expect China to do anything about distillation attacks on the frontier labs.
sentences.zh: 出于同样的原因，不要指望中国会对前沿实验室的蒸馏攻击采取任何行动。

sentences.en: I think it is mistaken to attribute all of the success of Chinese labs to distillation, but it’s just as much of a mistake to pretend like distillation doesn’t give Chinese labs a big advantage.
sentences.zh: 我认为将中国实验室的所有成功归因于蒸馏是错误的，但假装蒸馏并没有给中国实验室带来巨大优势也同样是错误的。

sentences.en: That advantage has really come to bear in the last year as post-training reinforcement learning has become increasingly crucial to model performance.
sentences.zh: 随着训练后强化学习对模型性能变得越来越重要，这一优势在去年才真正显现出来。

sentences.en: Instead of having to fashion reinforcement learning environments from scratch, Chinese labs can simply use frontier labs models as teachers, allowing for rapid improvement at much lower costs (this is not the only reason why Chinese models are cheaper to develop, but it’s a big one).
sentences.zh: 中国实验室不必从头开始打造强化学习环境，而是可以简单地使用前沿实验室模型作为教师，从而以更低的成本进行快速改进（这不是中国模型开发成本较低的唯一原因，但这是一个重要原因）。

blocks.html_en: What is interesting is that one of the most important use cases for Chinese models in the West is itself distillation. Thinking Machines, for example, which just released an open-weight model, relies on Chinese models to solve the cold start problem for reinforcement learning. Dean Meyer and Konstantine Buhler wrote an excellent article on X explaining that distillation means that Western open weight models are fundamentally disadvantaged relative to China:

sentences.en: What is interesting is that one of the most important use cases for Chinese models in the West is itself distillation.
sentences.zh: 有趣的是，中国模式在西方最重要的用例之一就是其本身的升华。

sentences.en: Thinking Machines, for example, which just released an open-weight model, relies on Chinese models to solve the cold start problem for reinforcement learning.
sentences.zh: 比如Thinking Machines刚刚发布了开放权重模型，就依靠中国模型来解决强化学习的冷启动问题。

sentences.en: Dean Meyer and Konstantine Buhler wrote an excellent article on X explaining that distillation means that Western open weight models are fundamentally disadvantaged relative to China:
sentences.zh: Dean Meyer 和 Konstantine Buhler 在 X 上写了一篇精彩的文章，解释了蒸馏意味着西方开放权重模型相对于中国从根本上处于劣势：

blocks.html_en:
Distillation does not explain China’s entire open-model lead. Chinese labs have world-class researchers, substantial compute, strong pre-trained models, software-hardware codesign, and rapidly improving post-training capabilities. But distillation compresses the costly final gap between a strong base and a near-frontier system. Even if distillation represents a smaller share of a Chinese model’s total capability, it represents a meaningful share of its advantage over American open models.

New enforcement mechanisms will make large-scale distillation harder, slower, and more expensive for Chinese companies. However, enforcement will not eliminate distillation backed by state actors. Every Western frontier advance therefore creates another teacher for Chinese labs. Western builders must either reproduce those capabilities independently or wait to learn from Chinese models. This gap gives Chinese labs a recurring structural advantage over Western companies.

sentences.en: Distillation does not explain China’s entire open-model lead.
sentences.zh: 蒸馏并不能解释中国在开放模式上的整体领先地位。

sentences.en: Chinese labs have world-class researchers, substantial compute, strong pre-trained models, software-hardware codesign, and rapidly improving post-training capabilities.

sentences.en: But distillation compresses the costly final gap between a strong base and a near-frontier system.
sentences.zh: 但蒸馏缩小了强大基础和近前沿系统之间代价高昂的最终差距。

sentences.en: Even if distillation represents a smaller share of a Chinese model’s total capability, it represents a meaningful share of its advantage over American open models.
sentences.zh: 即使蒸馏技术在中国模式的总能力中所占的份额较小，但与美国开放模式相比，它也代表了其明显的优势。

sentences.zh: 新的执行机制将使中国企业的大规模蒸馏变得更加困难、缓慢且成本更高。

sentences.en: However, enforcement will not eliminate distillation backed by state actors.
sentences.zh: 然而，执法并不会消除国家行为者支持的蒸馏。

sentences.zh: 因此，西方每一次前沿进步都会为中国实验室创造另一位老师。

sentences.zh: 西方建筑商必须要么独立复制这些能力，要么等待学习中国模式。

sentences.zh: 这种差距使中国实验室相对于西方公司具有持续的结构性优势。
